nodes.description: Agenesis or hypoplasia of the corpus callosum is a core CNS malformation in GPS and a key feature distinguishing it from milder KAT6B phenotypes.

evidence.reference_title: Genitopatellar syndrome (Orphanet structured-database record)
evidence.snippet: HP:0001274 | Agenesis of corpus callosum | Frequent (79-30%)

nodes.description: Congenital heart defects, most commonly atrial and ventricular septal defects, are common in GPS cohorts.

evidence.reference_title: KAT6B Disorders.
evidence.snippet: Congenital heart defects, small bowel malrotation, feeding difficulties, slow growth, cleft palate, hearing loss, and dental anomalies have been observed in individuals with either phenotype.

nodes.description: Loss of normal KAT6B-dependent histone acetylation and transcriptional coactivation results in epigenetic dysregulation of the limb/skeletal, urogenital, and brain developmental programs. Human pathological analyses and mouse expression studies point to systemic roles of KAT6B in controlling organismal growth and development, and CRISPR-engineered truncating-variant cell models show dysregulation of pathways aligned with the phenotype. This convergent developmental dysregulation produces the multisystem GPS phenotype (patellar aplasia, genital anomalies, corpus callosum agenesis, intellectual disability, renal anomalies).
nodes.id: Disrupted skeletal, genital, and CNS developmental programs

evidence.reference_title: Mutations in KAT6B, encoding a histone acetyltransferase, cause Genitopatellar syndrome.
evidence.snippet: Together, the data support an epigenetic dysregulation of the limb, brain, and genital developmental programs.

evidence.reference_title: Mutations in KAT6B, encoding a histone acetyltransferase, cause Genitopatellar syndrome.
evidence.snippet: Myst4 (the mouse orthologous gene) is expressed in mouse tissues corresponding to those affected by GPS.

evidence.reference_title: Novel variants in KAT6B spectrum of disorders expand our knowledge of clinical manifestations and molecular mechanisms.
evidence.snippet: We used CRISPR to introduce truncating variants into the KAT6B gene in model cell lines and performed chromatin accessibility and transcriptome sequencing to identify key dysregulated pathways.

nodes.description: KAT6B is a MYST-family histone acetyltransferase that acetylates histone H3 (including H3K23 and H3K9) within multiprotein chromatin-modifying complexes and acts as a transcriptional coactivator. GPS alleles reduce histone H3 and H4 acetylation in patient-derived cells and impair the transcriptional activation function of the protein, dysregulating expression of downstream developmental target genes (e.g., RUNX2, NR5A1). This is the proximate molecular lesion linking the KAT6B variant to abnormal developmental gene programs.
nodes.id: Dysregulation of histone acetylation and transcriptional coactivation

biological_processes: positive regulation of transcription by RNA polymerase II

evidence.reference_title: De novo mutations of the gene encoding the histone acetyltransferase KAT6B cause Genitopatellar syndrome.
evidence.snippet: We demonstrate a reduced level of both histone H3 and H4 acetylation in patient-derived cells suggesting that dysregulation of histone acetylation is a direct functional consequence of GPS alleles.

evidence.reference_title: Clinical features and underlying mechanisms of KAT6B disease in a Chinese boy.
evidence.snippet: RUNX2 and NR5A1, downstream products of the gene, affect the corresponding clinical symptoms.

nodes.description: Genital anomalies (e.g., scrotal hypoplasia, cryptorchidism, hypoplastic labia/clitoromegaly) are a hallmark of GPS and are more frequent and severe than in SBBYSS.

evidence.reference_title: Mutations in KAT6B, encoding a histone acetyltransferase, cause Genitopatellar syndrome.
evidence.snippet: By exome sequencing, we found de novo heterozygous truncating mutations in KAT6B (lysine acetyltransferase 6B, formerly known as MYST4 and MORF) in three subjects

evidence.reference_title: Genitopatellar Syndrome With a Novel Variant in the KAT6B Gene: Supporting Spectrum Delineation.
evidence.snippet: We present a case of an African American infant with classic GPS features and a novel KAT6B gene mutation (c.4066del, p.Glu1356Argfs*23).

nodes.description: GPS is caused by heterozygous truncating variants (predominantly frameshift and nonsense alleles) clustered in the terminal exon (exon 18) of KAT6B, most arising de novo. The variants are predicted to generate a truncated protein product lacking evolutionarily conserved C-terminal domains (including the transcriptional activation domain). Mutant transcripts escape nonsense-mediated decay and the truncated protein is expressed, supporting a dominant-negative or altered-function mechanism rather than simple haploinsufficiency.

evidence.reference_title: De novo mutations of the gene encoding the histone acetyltransferase KAT6B cause Genitopatellar syndrome.
evidence.snippet: All identified mutations are located within the terminal exon of the gene and are predicted to generate a truncated protein product lacking evolutionarily conserved domains.

evidence.reference_title: Mutations in KAT6B, encoding a histone acetyltransferase, cause Genitopatellar syndrome.

evidence.reference_title: Mutations in KAT6B, encoding a histone acetyltransferase, cause Genitopatellar syndrome.
evidence.snippet: The mutant transcripts do not undergo nonsense-mediated decay in cells from subjects with GPS.

nodes.description: Absent or hypoplastic patellae are the cardinal skeletal feature of GPS, typically more severe (aplasia/agenesis) than in the allelic SBBYSS.
